figures.gloss: Iuppiter — sky-father and sovereign of the Roman state, 'best and greatest'; his name the literal cognate of Zeus-pater.

edges.note: Iu-piter < *Dyēus-ph₂tēr — 'Sky-father' preserved almost intact in the compound.
Siblings, not borrowings: both descend straight from *Dyēus-ph₂tēr.
Dievas and Iuppiter (Dieus-pater) share the *Dyeus root by regular sound-law within Indo-European.
GOLD SEAM: Worshipped across the empire as Jupiter Ammon, blending the supreme Roman sovereign with the desert oracle.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The astrological planets are historically named for, and imbued with the exact temperaments of, the Roman pantheon.
The Etruscans' own equation: Tinia is the model and donor for Roman Jupiter, the Capitoline triad (Tinia-Uni-Menrva → Jupiter-Juno-Minerva) being the most direct Etruscan bequest to Rome.
Consort-pair within the Etruscan triad, mirroring the Tinia-Uni sovereignty couple at the head of the pantheon.
The succession theme reaches Rome through the Greek inheritance — Jupiter as heir of the same dethroning-the-elder pattern Hesiod drew from the Kumarbi cycle.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both derive from the same root of the daytime sky, though Jupiter evolved into a state-builder while Deipaturos remained a tribal elemental force.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Attested syncretism as 'Iuppiter Reve', matching the supreme Roman sky god with the indigenous sovereign of the peaks.
GOLD SEAM: Sol Invictus effectively supplanted Jupiter as the head of the Roman state pantheon in the late 3rd century, absorbing the role of the supreme protector of the state.
GOLD SEAM: Heavily syncretized in the Roman period as Jupiter Sabazius, merging the ultimate sky-father with the ecstatic mystery cult.

dossier.essence: The polytheistic state and civic religion of ancient Rome and its empire, from the archaic city (traditionally founded 753 BCE) through the Imperial period until public pagan cult was legally suppressed under Theodosius I (measures of 380–392 CE). It centered on maintaining the pax deorum — right relations between the community and its gods — through correct ritual, vow, and sacrifice. Religion was bound tightly to the political order, and correct performance mattered more than private belief.
dossier.cosmos: A vast, open pantheon headed by the Capitoline triad of Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva, with gods of state, place, and function and a divine power (numen) immanent in hearth, boundary, spring, and household (the Lares and Penates). Rome freely absorbed and identified foreign gods (interpretatio Romana), taking in Greek myth, Etruscan divinatory science, and later Eastern mystery cults (Cybele, Isis, Mithras) and the imperial cult of deified emperors. It was little concerned with systematic cosmology or a doctrinal afterlife: ideas of the dead (the Manes, the underworld) coexisted with philosophical and mystery-cult alternatives.
dossier.practice: Public religion was largely non-congregational — animal sacrifice, prayer, and vow performed for the community by magistrate-priests and colleges: the pontifices under the pontifex maximus, the augurs who read the auspices, the Vestal Virgins guarding the sacred fire, and the haruspices reading entrails in the Etruscan discipline. The Sibylline Books were consulted in crisis. Orthopraxy — exact performance — outweighed belief, and domestic cult honored the household gods and ancestors.
dossier.canon: No revealed scripture: religion rested on ritual tradition (the mos maiorum), priestly calendars and records (the fasti), and consulted collections such as the Sibylline Books. It is known largely through literary sources — Ovid's 'Fasti,' Livy, Cicero's 'On the Nature of the Gods' and 'On Divination,' and Varro's largely lost 'Antiquitates.'
dossier.reflects: Religion as the contractual upkeep of civic order — correct ritual reciprocity with the gods, fused to the state, where orthopraxy and the pax deorum outweigh belief or personal salvation.
sources: Mary Beard, John North, and Simon Price, 'Religions of Rome' (1998)
Robert Turcan, 'The Gods of Ancient Rome' (2000)
